paper_title: Propagation Model for Laser-Induced Harmonic Generation

methods.description: This method models the macroscopic harmonic response generated by an intense laser pulse focused into a gaseous medium. It involves numerically integrating Maxwell's wave equations with source terms distributed across the medium, considering the separation of the electric field into driving IR laser and generated XUV components.

steps.description: Numerically integrate Maxwell's wave equations with source terms distributed across the extended medium.
steps.searchable_summary: integrate Maxwell's wave equations for laser propagation in gas

steps.description: Apply the slowly evolving wave approximation (SEWA) to simplify Maxwell's equations in frequency space.
steps.searchable_summary: apply SEWA to simplify Maxwell's equations

steps.description: Separate the total electric field into the driving IR laser field and the generated XUV field, solving separately for each component.
steps.searchable_summary: separate and solve for IR and XUV fields

steps.description: Consider atomic gas densities and interaction lengths to ignore linear dispersion and absorption effects for the driving field.
steps.searchable_summary: ignore linear dispersion for driving field

steps.description: Include the nonlinear dipole response of the molecular gas and XUV absorption by neutral atoms.
steps.searchable_summary: include nonlinear dipole response and XUV absorption

steps.description: Calculate the far-field response analytically in the frequency domain using a Huygen's integral.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate far-field response using Huygen's integral

steps.description: Analyze the phase matching conditions by changing the gas jet position or density profile to select specific harmonic contributions.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze phase matching by adjusting gas jet parameters
